output:
# Research Analysis: TACT -- Tail-Adaptive, Calibrated-Trust Global Alignment for Federated Long-Tailed Learning

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

The source is a METHOD paper in cross-device federated learning (FL) under data
heterogeneity and a long-tailed aggregate label distribution: it places a
Multimodal LLM (MLLM) SOLELY on the server to emit a "global alignment" signal
that corrects the client classifier's bias toward head classes, WITHOUT
transmitting gradients or federated features to the server (a strictly stronger
privacy posture than CReFF, and cheaper on clients than CLIP2FL). We name the
load-bearing weakness FROST -- Frozen, Reliability-unaware, One-Size,
Static-Trust alignment -- because that signal is frozen on three axes at once:
the same for every client (ignoring local skew), static across rounds (ignoring
evolving feature geometry), and uniformly trusting the MLLM for every class
(ignoring that MLLM reliability is itself long-tailed, so rare classes get the
least reliable anchor at a fixed strength). The reframe: that alignment signal
is not a fixed prior but ONE limiting setting of a learned operator. We propose
TACT -- Tail-Adaptive, Calibrated-Trust alignment -- with three identity-default
knobs: per-client strength a_k = 2*sigmoid(w^T s_k), per-class trust
tau_c = sigmoid(u*r_c+v), and an optional round budget gamma_t = beta_src*exp(phi^T d_t),
composed as align(k,c) = gamma_t * a_k * tau_c * A_c. At the off settings
(a_k=1, tau_c=1, gamma_t=beta_src) TACT provably recomputes the source's fixed
alignment within floating-point tolerance (max abs diff <= 1e-6) at init with no
training -- a B1 do-no-harm collapse. Throughout, collapse identities are tagged
[provable-by-construction] and every accuracy/fairness gain is tagged
[conjectured -> measured].

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Federated learning (FL) trains a shared model across many clients that keep
their data local, aggregating only model updates rather than raw examples; the
canonical procedure is FedAvg [1]. Its central difficulty is statistical
heterogeneity: when client data are non-IID, local updates drift apart and the
averaged model degrades, a failure mode documented early [2] and catalogued as
a core open problem of the field [3]. Two influential responses keep clients
near a shared reference -- FedProx [4] adds a proximal term to bound local
drift, and SCAFFOLD [5] uses control variates to correct client-update bias.
These methods stabilize optimization under heterogeneity, but they treat all
classes symmetrically; none specifically counters the systematic bias that
arises when the aggregate label distribution is long-tailed, which is the
regime this paper targets.

Long-tailed recognition in the CENTRAL setting supplies the key structural
insight that motivates a server-side classifier signal. A long line of work --
re-margining and re-weighting via LDAM-DRW [7], loss re-balancing via Balanced
Softmax [8], and open-world memory via OLTR [9] -- improves tail accuracy, but
the decisive observation is the DECOUPLING result of Kang et al. [6]: under
class imbalance the learned REPRESENTATION is already nearly balanced, and
long-tail bias concentrates almost entirely in the CLASSIFIER head. Simply
re-balancing or re-training the classifier (cRT) on a fixed backbone recovers
most of the lost tail accuracy. Surveys of the area consolidate this
classifier-versus-representation framing [10]. This is precisely why a
server-side signal that re-aligns the CLASSIFIER -- rather than re-training
representations or touching client compute -- is the right lever for long-tail
correction in FL.

Federated long-tailed methods combine these two threads, and each pays a
distinct cost. CReFF [11] re-trains the classifier on the server using
FEDERATED FEATURES synthesized from client gradients -- effective, but it
transmits gradient-derived feature information to the server, opening a leakage
surface. CLIP2FL [12] injects foundation-model knowledge by running CLIP to
guide federated long-tail training, but it places the heavy vision-language
model ON THE CLIENTS, imposing edge compute that cross-device deployments can
rarely afford. Related lines share one or both costs: FEDIC [13] calibrates via
distillation over logits, BalanceFL [14] rebalances local training, Fed-GraB
[15] self-adjusts a gradient balancer, and classifier-calibration approaches
such as CCVR [16] post-hoc correct the head using transmitted feature
statistics. Across this family, correction is bought either by moving
gradients/features to the server (privacy cost) or by moving heavy models to
the clients (compute cost) -- and, critically, when a foundation-model prior is
used it is treated as a FIXED, uniformly trusted, client-agnostic anchor.

The no-gradient-transmission posture is not cosmetic: it is justified by a
mature attack literature. Deep Leakage from Gradients (DLG) [17] showed that
raw training samples can be reconstructed from shared gradients alone; iDLG [18]
sharpened this by recovering labels analytically; and Inverting Gradients [19]
demonstrated high-fidelity image recovery even in realistic FL settings. Any
method that ships gradients or gradient-derived features to the server (as CReFF
does) inherits this exposure. A method that adds correction WITHOUT introducing
new gradient/feature transmission therefore occupies a strictly stronger privacy
position, which is exactly the source method's posture and the one TACT must
preserve.

The server-side knowledge itself comes from foundation and multimodal models.
CLIP [20] provides zero-shot, language-grounded class semantics, and MLLMs such
as LLaVA [21] and BLIP-2 [22] extend this to instruction-following multimodal
reasoning -- making them attractive as a SERVER-RESIDENT source of "what each
class should look like." But foundation-model outputs are not uniformly
trustworthy. Modern networks are badly miscalibrated, with confidence decoupled
from correctness [23], and vision-language models hallucinate and degrade
precisely on rare or unusual concepts [24]. The consequence is a cruel
structural mismatch: an MLLM's zero-shot reliability is ITSELF long-tailed, so
the rare classes that most need a trustworthy anchor receive the LEAST reliable
one. This grounds the per-class trust knob -- correction should be DISCOUNTED
exactly where the prior is weakest.

Putting the lineage together locates the gap precisely. FL stabilizers [4,5]
do not address long-tail bias; central decoupling [6] says the classifier is
the right target but says nothing about FL privacy/compute; federated long-tail
methods [11,12,13,14,15,16] either transmit gradients/features [11,16] or load
clients with heavy models [12]; and the source method avoids both by emitting
its alignment signal from a server-side MLLM. Yet that signal is FROST: frozen
to be the same for every client (ignoring local skew), static across rounds
(ignoring evolving feature geometry), and uniformly trusting the MLLM for every
class (ignoring that MLLM reliability [23,24] is itself long-tailed). This
alignment signal is the ONLY force countering long-tail bias, so all accuracy
and fairness rest on it -- yet no method above makes it client-adaptive,
geometry-aware, AND reliability-calibrated at once. That triple-frozen,
load-bearing signal is the gap TACT closes.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

TACT is an input-conditioned modulation of the source's single fixed alignment
signal. It introduces NO new GRADIENT OR FEATURE transmission: every input it
consumes is either already at the server (class-level MLLM semantics, the
server's own global model state) or is low-sensitivity, DP-noisable per-class
label-count metadata of the kind long-tail FL methods already collect.

PRIVACY NOTE (by construction): s_k is built ONLY from the per-class label-count
metadata that long-tailed FL baselines already require clients to share
(optionally DP-noised); TACT requests no new raw data, no gradients, and no
federated features -- so the source's no-gradient-transmission privacy posture
is preserved by construction.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEFINITIONS (all server-side)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

  A_c  -- SOURCE class-c anchor (given, frozen by the source method).
          The MLLM-derived class-c semantic prototype: the text/embedding
          class center the MLLM emits for class c (e.g., normalized class
          name / attribute prompt embedding), L2-normalized in the global
          classifier's feature space. The source aligns the global linear
          head W = [W_1 ... W_C] toward {A_c} with a single strength beta_src:
              align_src(c) = beta_src * A_c       (same for every client k,
                                                    every round t, trust = 1).

  s_k  -- LOCAL SKEW FEATURE for client k (KNOB 1 input). Built ONLY from the
          per-class label-count vector n_k = [n_{k,1} ... n_{k,C}] that
          long-tail FL methods already share (or its DP-noised version):
              s_k = [ log(1 + n_k) normalized to unit sum,          # shape
                      imbalance_ratio_k = max_c n_{k,c} / min_c+ n_{k,c},
                      H(n_k) = normalized label entropy,            # skew
                      tail_mass_k = sum_{c in Few} n_{k,c} / sum n_k ]
          dim(s_k) = C + 3 (or a fixed small summary; see compactness note).

  r_c  -- MLLM RELIABILITY SCORE for class c (KNOB 2 input), computed WITHOUT
          any client data. PRIMARY estimator -- public-probe self-consistency:
          on a small PUBLIC, label-free probe set X_probe (e.g., a held-out
          slice of a public corpus, NOT client data), query the MLLM K times
          with prompt paraphrases / temperature for class c and measure
          agreement + confidence margin:
              r_c = mean over X_probe of [ p_top1(c-query) - p_top2 ]   margin
                    x  consistency(c)                                   K-vote
          High r_c = MLLM is sharp and self-consistent on class c; low r_c =
          MLLM is uncertain/contradictory (typical for rare tail concepts).
          ALTERNATIVE estimator -- prototype-agreement: cosine between the MLLM
          textual prototype A_c and a class-c prototype mu_c computed SERVER-SIDE
          ONLY -- either the global classifier head's normalized weight vector
          W_c, or the global model run on the PUBLIC, label-free probe set --
          NEVER from client data or transmitted client features; r_c =
          cos(A_c, mu_c). (Both are pure functions of server-held quantities; an
          ORACLE r_c is used as an upper bound in ablations, see Q5.)

  d_t  -- GLOBAL GEOMETRY DRIFT summary at round t (KNOB 3 input, optional):
          d_t = [ mean_c || mu_c^(t) - mu_c^(t-1) ||,   centroid motion
                  ang. change of head W^(t) vs W^(t-1) ]   -- server-only
          (mu_c is the SAME server-side prototype defined above: global-head /
          public-probe derived, NEVER client features).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
THE THREE KNOBS (identity-default = pass-through)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

  KNOB 1  per-CLIENT tail-adaptive strength:
            a_k = 2 * sigmoid( w^T s_k )            # w in R^{dim(s_k)}
            init  w = 0   ->  a_k = 1  for all k    (source uniform strength)

  KNOB 2  per-CLASS calibrated trust:
            tau_c = sigmoid( u * r_c + v )          # u, v scalars
            init  u = 0, v -> +inf (clamped)  ->  tau_c = 1  for all c (full trust)

  KNOB 3  (optional) ROUND/geometry budget:
            gamma_t = beta_src * exp( phi^T d_t )    # phi in R^{dim(d_t)}
            init  phi = 0   ->  gamma_t = beta_src   (source static schedule)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMPOSED CORRECTION (applied to the server's global classifier head)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

            align(k, c) = gamma_t * a_k * tau_c * A_c

  Applied server-side, two equivalent injection points (report the head form):
    (i) HEAD form: W_c <- W_c + align(k,c)   when forming/refining the global
        head for/with client k's update (anchor pull on class-c weight vector).
    (ii) LOGIT form: z_c <- z_c + <align(k,c), f(x)>   a server-side logit
         correction toward the trusted anchor.
  No client raw data, no client gradients, no client features are required to
  compute align(k,c): a_k uses shared label counts; tau_c uses a public probe /
  server centroids; gamma_t uses the server's own model trajectory.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
PARAMETER COUNT (supports "no extra client compute")
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

  |w| = dim(s_k)  (= C + 3, or a fixed compact summary -> O(1) if s_k is the
        4 scalar summary [imbalance_ratio, entropy, tail_mass, head_mass]),
  u, v = 2 scalars,  phi = dim(d_t) (~2 scalars).
  TOTAL trainable: a few to (C+5) scalars, ALL on the SERVER. Clients run the
  IDENTICAL FL model and protocol as the source -- zero extra client compute,
  zero extra client memory, zero extra transmitted bytes. The knobs are trained
  by the server on server-held metadata + the public probe set.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
COLLAPSE (B1)  [provable-by-construction]
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

  OFF point: w = 0 (a_k = 1), u = 0 & v->+inf (tau_c = 1), phi = 0 (gamma_t =
  beta_src). Substituting:
            align(k,c) = beta_src * 1 * 1 * A_c = align_src(c)   for all k, c, t.
  B1 unit test, run at INIT with NO training:
            assert  max_{k,c} | align_TACT(k,c) - align_src(c) | <= 1e-6
  Insertion provably cannot change source behavior at the off knobs (do-no-harm
  floor). FedAvg recovered too: beta_src = 0 (or gamma_t = 0) -> no corrective
  force, base aggregator. CReFF-style classifier-retraining is recovered as a
  configuration by swapping the anchor source A_c for federated re-trained class
  features under uniform trust (tau_c = 1) and uniform strength (a_k = 1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
CLAIM TAGGING (kept distinct throughout)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

  [provable-by-construction]  -- the B1 collapse identity (a_k=1, tau_c=1,
        gamma_t=beta_src reproduce the source within 1e-6, no training). This
        is an algebraic property of the operator, asserted by unit test.
  [conjectured -> measured]   -- ALL accuracy / fairness / calibration gains
        (tail accuracy up, worst-class up, per-class STD down, tail ECE down).
        These are hypotheses confirmed or refuted ONLY by the experiments in
        Q5; they are never implied by the collapse identity.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

1. PROVE B1 BEFORE ANY ACCURACY CLAIM (pass/fail gate). The very first
   experiment is the no-training collapse unit test: instantiate TACT at the
   off knobs and assert max_{k,c} |align_TACT - align_src| <= 1e-6 on every
   benchmark's anchor set. This is a HARD GATE -- if it fails, the operator is
   mis-specified and no downstream number is reportable. The do-no-harm floor
   is established here, [provable-by-construction], independent of any tuning.

2. VALIDATE EACH KNOB CARRIES SEPARABLE, NON-REDUNDANT SIGNAL (factorial +
   placebo). Run the full on/off CUBE over {a_k, tau_c, gamma_t} (8 cells) to
   show each knob's marginal contribution and that they are not mutually
   redundant. Then run PLACEBOS that preserve capacity but destroy the
   conditioning: (a) RANDOM per-class trust -- a tau_c drawn to match the
   learned tau_c's MEAN and variance but uncorrelated with r_c; (b)
   SHUFFLED-skew per-client strength -- a_k computed on s_k permuted across
   clients. If TACT does not beat these placebos, the mechanism is INERT and
   any gain is a global re-scaling, not adaptation. Gains MUST survive both.

3. CAPACITY / COMPUTE-MATCHED CONTROL + ANTI-CLOCK LEAKAGE CHECK. The win must
   not be "more parameters." Compare TACT against the source method augmented
   with an equal-sized but FIXED (untrained) perturbation of the same shape
   (constant a_k, tau_c fed by the same number of free scalars, no
   conditioning). Separately, prove the strength knob learned more than the
   obvious statistic: regress the learned a_k on the raw per-client imbalance
   ratio; a_k must have a NON-TRIVIAL RESIDUAL after removing the
   imbalance-ratio component, and that residual must correlate with held-out
   TAIL accuracy. If a_k is just an imbalance-ratio clock, the residual is
   noise and the conditioning adds nothing.

4. CLAIM DISCIPLINE. Tag every number. The do-no-harm collapse is
   [provable-by-construction] (B1 unit test). EVERY accuracy, worst-class,
   per-class-STD, and ECE figure is [conjectured -> measured] -- reported only
   with the matched-compute baseline, the placebo controls, and the non-IID
   severity sweep alongside it. No leaderboard delta is allowed to stand in
   for mechanism evidence; mechanism is proven by placebo + capacity-match +
   the tau_c-low-where-r_c-low diagnostic, not by a single headline win.

TRANSFERABLE INSIGHT. The reusable contribution is larger than long-tail
classifiers: the pattern is "replace a frozen, uniformly-trusted foundation-
model prior with a learned, calibrated, identity-default operator that collapses
exactly to the prior." Any FL system that injects server-side foundation-model
knowledge -- prompt-tuning servers, distillation teachers, retrieval anchors,
synthetic-data generators -- currently hard-wires that knowledge as a fixed,
uniformly trusted signal. The TACT recipe (condition strength on per-client
need, discount trust by per-class model reliability, and initialize every knob
to pass-through so insertion is provably do-no-harm) turns "trust the foundation
model" from an untestable assumption into a falsifiable, strictly-generalizing
operator -- a template other server-side-knowledge FL methods can adopt directly.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Here are the five core questions:

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can replacing the source method's fixed, client-agnostic, MLLM-reliability-unaware server-side global alignment signal (the FROST gap) with TACT -- a learned operator that sets a per-client tail-adaptive alignment strength conditioned on local long-tailed skew (a_k = 2*sigmoid(w^T s_k), init w=0 so a_k=1) and a per-class anchor trust conditioned on MLLM calibration/reliability (tau_c = sigmoid(u*r_c+v), init u=0,v->inf so tau_c=1), composed as align(k,c)=beta_src*a_k*tau_c*A_c -- measurably improve tail/minority-class accuracy and per-class fairness on standard federated long-tailed benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10-LT, CIFAR-100-LT, ImageNet-LT under non-IID client splits) while, at the off settings a_k=1 and tau_c=1, provably recomputing the source's fixed global alignment within floating-point tolerance (max abs diff <= 1e-6) at initialization with no training?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Long-tail fairness in FL is not a benchmark curiosity -- it sits on the path of
safety-critical edge applications. Cross-device FL is the natural training
regime for fleets of phones, vehicles, and clinical devices, and these are
exactly the settings where rare classes carry the highest stakes: an
autonomous-driving model must recognize the rare pedestrian-in-a-wheelchair or
overturned-truck event, and a federated medical model must flag the uncommon
pathology, not just the common findings. Aggregate accuracy hides these
failures; per-class and worst-class accuracy expose them. A method that
specifically raises TAIL and worst-class accuracy under non-IID client splits,
without weakening the privacy or compute guarantees that made FL deployable in
the first place, therefore addresses a real reliability-and-fairness gap rather
than a leaderboard delta.

The deeper interest is conceptual: it changes HOW the community adds
foundation-model knowledge to FL. Today that knowledge enters as a FIXED prior
-- a hand-set strength, applied identically everywhere, trusted uniformly. TACT
reframes the alignment signal from a frozen prior into a LEARNED, calibrated
OPERATOR: adaptive to each client's long-tail skew, discounted where the MLLM
is unreliable, and equipped with an exact do-no-harm collapse. The headline is
that collapse identity -- at its identity-default knobs (a_k=1, tau_c=1,
gamma_t=beta_src) TACT provably recomputes the source signal within
floating-point tolerance (max abs diff <= 1e-6) with no training. This converts
"trust the foundation model" from an untestable assumption into a FALSIFIABLE,
strictly-generalizing operator: the source method and FedAvg both reappear as
configurations, so any measured gain is gain OVER a provable equality, not over
an unverified baseline.

The practical payoff makes it adoptable. Every TACT knob is server-side and
consumes only information the server already has -- class metadata, an
MLLM-reliability proxy, and low-sensitivity label-count skew statistics that FL
long-tail methods already share. There is NO extra client compute and NO new
gradient or feature transmission, so the source's privacy posture [17,18,19] and
client-efficiency advantage [12] are both preserved by construction. The result
is a drop-in upgrade: better tail and per-class fairness with the same client
footprint, layered onto existing FL aggregators [1,4,5] rather than replacing
them. The improvements demonstrated here are on standard academic benchmarks;
TACT is a research prototype, and safety-critical deployment (autonomous driving,
clinical) requires domain-specific validation, regulatory compliance, and
adversarial-robustness testing beyond this work's scope.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The first hard problem is ESTIMATING PER-CLASS MLLM RELIABILITY r_c WITHOUT
CLIENT DATA AND WITHOUT A LABELED TAIL VALIDATION SET. The whole point of
KNOB 2 is to DOWN-WEIGHT the MLLM exactly on the rare classes where it is least
trustworthy -- but those are precisely the classes for which we have the least
evidence at the server. A naive "use the MLLM's own confidence" estimator is
self-defeating: MLLM confidence is itself miscalibrated on the tail (it is
often over-confident on rare-but-distinctive concepts and under-confident on
fine-grained ones), so reading r_c off raw softmax would mis-rank the very
classes the knob exists to protect. We are forced to a calibration proxy
computed on a PUBLIC, label-free probe set (self-consistency across prompt
paraphrases, or agreement between the MLLM prototype and the current global
centroid), and we must demonstrate -- via the ORACLE r_c upper bound -- how
much of the achievable fairness gain a realizable estimator actually captures.

The second obstacle is that PER-CLIENT ADAPTATION MUST NOT CREATE NEW PRIVACY
LEAKAGE. The source's entire selling point versus CReFF/CLIP2FL is that it
transmits NO gradients or federated features. KNOB 1 therefore cannot consume
anything richer than the low-sensitivity per-class label counts already shared
by long-tail FL methods (optionally DP-noised). This STARVES the strength knob
of signal BY DESIGN: a_k must extract a useful per-client correction from a
coarse, possibly noisy, count histogram and nothing else. There is a genuine
tension -- more conditioning signal would help accuracy but would violate the
privacy invariant that defines the method's contribution. Relatedly,
NON-STATIONARITY makes a one-shot anchor drift: the global feature geometry
moves every round as clients update, so a static A_c (and a static strength)
goes stale -- yet KNOB 3 must track that drift using only the server's own
model trajectory d_t, never client gradients.

The deepest obstacle is CONFOUNDING. Any measured gain could come from added
capacity, from incidental regularization, or from a trivial re-weighting clock
that merely scales correction by class count -- none of which is the claimed
mechanism (calibrated, skew-conditioned adaptation). This is why naive
approaches fail and must be excluded explicitly: "just trust the MLLM MORE on
the tail" amplifies exactly the anchors that are least reliable there, pulling
minority boundaries toward a wrong prior and HURTING worst-class accuracy;
"just re-balance by class count" is the imbalance-ratio clock -- it ignores
whether the anchor is trustworthy at all and collapses to standard logit
adjustment, which the source already subsumes. Proving TACT therefore requires
placebo controls and capacity matching, not a leaderboard delta: the
contribution is real only if the conditioning -- and specifically tau_c being
LOW where r_c is low -- is what moves the tail.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The barrier is that every prior route to long-tail correction in FL spends one
of two currencies the deployment cannot easily afford. Methods like CReFF [11]
and feature-calibration approaches [16] buy correction with PRIVACY: they move
gradients or gradient-derived feature statistics to the server, and the
gradient-inversion literature [17,18,19] shows that this is exactly the channel
that leaks raw data. Methods like CLIP2FL [12] buy correction with COMPUTE:
they place a large vision-language model on the clients, which cross-device
edge hardware cannot sustain. The source method escapes both by keeping the
MLLM server-side and transmitting no gradients -- but it then takes the cheap
path of treating the foundation-model prior as a single FIXED, uniformly
trusted, client-agnostic anchor. So the design space had a server-side,
privacy-preserving corner, but nobody had made the signal in that corner
adaptive.

Why was the adaptive version overlooked? Two assumptions quietly foreclosed it.
First, if you treat the MLLM prior as ground truth, its OWN long-tailed
reliability is invisible: there is no reason to discount any class, so a
per-class trust term never arises -- even though calibration [23] and
hallucination [24] results say the prior is weakest precisely on the rare
classes that need it most. Second, per-client adaptation LOOKS like it requires
leaking each client's distribution to the server, which would forfeit the very
privacy advantage the server-side design was protecting. Both assumptions are
wrong in this setting: MLLM reliability can be probed server-side from
held-out anchors, and per-client adaptation needs only label-count metadata
that FL long-tail methods [11,16] already exchange. Once those two false
constraints are dropped, the missing idea is simply that the alignment signal
should be LEARNED and CALIBRATED -- stronger for clients with worse skew,
weaker where the MLLM is untrustworthy.

What distinguishes TACT from "yet another heuristic re-weighting" is the
identity-default construction plus the exact collapse. Each knob is
parameterized to default to pass-through -- a_k = 2*sigmoid(w^T s_k) with w=0 so
a_k=1, tau_c = sigmoid(u*r_c+v) initialized so tau_c=1, and gamma_t initialized
to beta_src -- and at those defaults align(k,c) reduces EXACTLY to the source's
beta_src * A_c. That makes TACT a strict do-no-harm GENERALIZATION: insertion
provably cannot change the source's behavior before training, and the source
method and FedAvg are recovered as named configurations. Prior re-weighting
schemes offer no such guarantee; they replace one heuristic with another and
must be argued empirically from scratch. TACT instead starts from a proven
equality and asks only whether learning to deviate from it -- adaptively and
calibratedly -- helps the tail, a question that capacity-matched and
shuffled-feature placebo controls can answer cleanly.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD recap. TACT is a SERVER-SIDE DROP-IN over the source method's fixed MLLM
global alignment. It replaces the single static signal align_src(c) =
beta_src * A_c with align(k,c) = gamma_t * a_k * tau_c * A_c, where three tiny
knobs -- per-client strength a_k (conditioned on shared label-count skew s_k),
per-class trust tau_c (conditioned on server-estimated MLLM reliability r_c),
and an optional round budget gamma_t (conditioned on global geometry drift d_t)
-- all default to identity (a_k=1, tau_c=1, gamma_t=beta_src) so the source is
recovered exactly. The knobs are trained ON THE SERVER using only already-shared
metadata and a small public probe set; clients keep the identical FL model,
identical protocol, and transmit no extra bytes.

DATASETS (real).
- CIFAR-10-LT and CIFAR-100-LT, imbalance factors 100 / 50 / 10.
- ImageNet-LT (large-scale long tail).
- iNaturalist-2018 (natural, fine-grained long tail).
- FEMNIST (cross-device realism: many small, naturally non-IID clients).
- FEDERATED SPLIT PROTOCOL: Dirichlet(alpha) non-IID partition over clients
  (alpha in {0.05, 0.1, 0.5} for a severity sweep), with a per-client long-tail
  imposed on top so each client sees its own skewed slice of the global tail.

BASELINES (real).
- General FL: FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD.
- Long-tail FL: CReFF, CLIP2FL, and THE SOURCE METHOD ITSELF (TACT must collapse
  to it via B1; it is both baseline and collapse target). Privacy contrast:
  CReFF/CLIP2FL transmit gradients/features or put CLIP on clients; TACT does
  neither.
- Central decoupling SKYLINES (upper-reference, not FL): cRT, LDAM-DRW,
  Balanced Softmax -- to bound how much tail accuracy is recoverable at all.

METRICS.
- Overall top-1 accuracy.
- Many / Medium / Few-shot split accuracy; HEADLINE = Few (tail) accuracy.
- WORST-CLASS accuracy AND WORST-20%-CLASS accuracy (the hardest fairness
  targets).
- Per-class accuracy STD and head-tail accuracy GAP (fairness).
- Calibration: ECE, reported specifically on the TAIL bucket (tail-bucket ECE).
- Communication cost + CLIENT compute / memory -- must be UNCHANGED vs source.
- B1 COLLAPSE-FIDELITY: max_{k,c} |align_TACT - align_src| at the off knobs.
  This is the CORE NEW METRIC -- the do-no-harm floor, reported first.
- PRIVACY INVARIANT (reported, not just asserted): s_k is built ONLY from the
  per-class label-count metadata that long-tailed FL baselines already require
  clients to share (optionally DP-noised); TACT requests no new raw data, no
  gradients, and no federated features -- so the source's no-gradient-transmission
  privacy posture is preserved by construction.
- SCOPE NOTE: benchmark long-tail (CIFAR-LT, ImageNet-LT, iNaturalist) measures
  class-frequency imbalance and is NOT a claim about fairness on protected human
  attributes, which requires dedicated evaluation beyond this scope.

ABLATIONS.
- B1 collapse recovery (no-training init unit test; the pass/fail gate).
- KNOB FACTORIAL: all 8 on/off cells of {a_k, tau_c, gamma_t}.
- PLACEBO: random per-class trust (mean/variance-matched, r_c-uncorrelated) and
  shuffled-skew per-client strength -- gains must survive both.
- CAPACITY / COMPUTE-MATCHED control: source + equal-size fixed (untrained)
  perturbation, no conditioning.
- RELIABILITY-ESTIMATOR variants: public-probe self-consistency vs
  prototype-agreement, plus an ORACLE r_c (perfect tail reliability = upper
  bound on the trust knob's achievable gain).
- LEAKAGE / ANTI-CLOCK regression: regress a_k on raw imbalance ratio; report
  the residual and its correlation with held-out tail accuracy.
- NON-IID SEVERITY sweep over Dirichlet alpha.
- ROBUSTNESS to MLLM choice (swap the server MLLM; gains should persist).

EXPECTED OUTCOMES (each tagged).
- B1 passes: max_{k,c} |align_TACT - align_src| <= 1e-6 at init, no training.
  [provable-by-construction]
- TACT improves Few-shot and worst-class accuracy and lowers per-class STD and
  tail ECE over the source AT MATCHED CLIENT COMPUTE and ZERO extra gradient
  transmission. [conjectured -> measured]
- Mechanism evidence: tau_c is LOW exactly where MLLM reliability r_c is low
  (the tail), and discounting the anchor there is what drives the fairness gain
  (oracle r_c upper-bounds it; estimator variants approach it).
  [conjectured -> measured]
- Placebo (random/shuffled knobs) and capacity-matched controls show ~no gain,
  proving the win is the MECHANISM (conditioning), not added capacity. The
  anti-clock regression shows a_k carries residual signal beyond imbalance
  ratio that correlates with tail accuracy. [conjectured -> measured]
